metadata.title: Science Without Memory
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The problem is a memory crisis: papers report the destination, not the path taken. Institutional memory is dynamic, contextual, and relational, not a static archive. In AI research, continuity breaks constantly, so reasoning like why a learning rate was chosen disappears. The deeper problem isn't missing files but lost context that no artifact preserved.

artifactLines: The problem is a memory crisis: papers report the destination, not the path taken.
Institutional memory is dynamic, contextual, and relational, not a static archive.
In AI research, continuity breaks constantly, so reasoning like why a learning rate was chosen disappears.
The deeper problem isn't missing files but lost context that no artifact preserved.

beats.narration_text: The reframing that a paper only reports the destination while the real reasoning, like why a learning rate was chosen, vanishes hit home as a memory crisis, not a missing-files problem. I want to rebuild that lost context. For one experiment I ran, (1) reconstruct the dynamic, relational institutional memory a paper would omit, (2) capture the decision trail behind one hyperparameter choice, and (3) show what continuity practice would keep that reasoning alive for whoever picks it up next.

I want to rebuild that lost context. For one experiment I ran, (1) reconstruct the dynamic, relational institutional memory a paper would omit, (2) capture the decision trail behind one hyperparameter choice, and (3) show what continuity practice would keep that reasoning alive for whoever picks it up next.
output: The problem is not that AI researchers are careless
Consider what scientific memory actually requires

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'Science Without Memory' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.
